Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \sec^{-1}\left(\frac{2u}{7}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 7, \quad y = \sqrt{4 \, u^{2} - 49}, \quad r = 2u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{4 \, u^{2} - 49}}{2u}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{7}{2u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{4 \, u^{2} - 49}}{2u}\right)\left(\frac{7}{2u}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{7 \, \sqrt{4 \, u^{2} - 49}}{2 \, u^{2}}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{3u}{3}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 3u, \quad y = \sqrt{-9 \, u^{2} + 9}, \quad r = 3

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{-9 \, u^{2} + 9}}{3}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{3u}{3}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{-9 \, u^{2} + 9}}{3}\right)\left(\frac{3u}{3}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{2 \, \sqrt{-9 \, u^{2} + 9} u}{3}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \csc^{-1}\left(\frac{5u}{10}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 5 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}, \quad y = 10, \quad r = 5u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{10}{5u}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{5 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}}{5u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{10}{5u}\right)\left(\frac{5 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}}{5u}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{4 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}}{u^{2}}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \csc^{-1}\left(4u\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = \sqrt{16 \, u^{2} - 1}, \quad y = 1, \quad r = 4u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{1}{4u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\left(\frac{1}{4u}\right)^2

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \frac{8 \, u^{2} - 1}{8 \, u^{2}}

\cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\left(3u\right)^2

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\cos(2\alpha) = -18 \, u^{2} + 1

\tan(2\alpha) = \frac{2\left(\frac{8}{\sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 64}}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{8}{\sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 64}}\right)^2}

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\tan(2\alpha) = \frac{16 \, \sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 64}}{9 \, u^{2} - 128}

\text{Let } \alpha = \sec^{-1}\left(\frac{3u}{10}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 10, \quad y = \sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 100}, \quad r = 3u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 100}}{10}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\tan(2\alpha) = \tan(2\alpha) = \frac{2\tan(\alpha)}{1 - \tan^2(\alpha)}

\tan(2\alpha) = \frac{2\left(\frac{\sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 100}}{10}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{\sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 100}}{10}\right)^2}

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\tan(2\alpha) = -\frac{20 \, \sqrt{9 \, u^{2} - 100}}{9 \, u^{2} - 200}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{2u}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 4, \quad y = 2 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}, \quad r = 2u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{2 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}}{2u}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{4}{2u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{2 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}}{2u}\right)\left(\frac{4}{2u}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{4 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 4}}{u^{2}}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{25}{u}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = \sqrt{u^{2} - 625}, \quad y = 25, \quad r = u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{25}{u}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{u^{2} - 625}}{u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{25}{u}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{u^{2} - 625}}{u}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{50 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 625}}{u^{2}}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \csc^{-1}\left(u\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = \sqrt{u^{2} - 1}, \quad y = 1, \quad r = u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{1}{u}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{u^{2} - 1}}{u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{1}{u}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{u^{2} - 1}}{u}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{2 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 1}}{u^{2}}

\cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\left(\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^{2} + 64}}\right)^2
